questions.question: In the AOSA chapter's six design principles, adding more servers to handle capacity most directly improves scalability but commonly trades off against which other principles?
options: Manageability and cost (more machines to operate and pay for)
Availability and reliability (more servers always reduce uptime)
Performance and latency (horizontal scale always slows responses)
Security and compliance (distributed systems cannot be audited)
questions.explanation: AOSA states that scaling by adding servers improves capacity but increases operational burden (manageability) and hardware/TCO (cost). The principles are often in tension—not mutually exclusive.

questions.question: High availability in a distributed web architecture primarily requires which combination of strategies?
options: Redundancy for key components, rapid partial-failure recovery, and graceful degradation
Eliminating all single points of failure before launch, even at prototype scale
Maximizing strong consistency across every read path
Vertical scaling of one super-node until capacity is sufficient
questions.explanation: AOSA defines availability as uptime through redundancy, fast recovery from partial failures, and graceful degradation—not perfection or a single giant machine.

questions.question: Which statement best captures 'reliability' as defined in the scalable web architecture principles?
options: A read returns consistent data for the same request, and updates persist for future retrieval
Every request completes in under 100 ms regardless of load
The system never loses a network packet between datacenters
All replicas agree on every write before any client receives a response
questions.explanation: Reliability in AOSA is about dependable, consistent data: repeated reads return the same value, and stored data remains available after writes.

questions.question: Why does the image-hosting example split read and write paths into separate services?
options: Writes hold connections open longer than reads, so a combined endpoint can exhaust connection limits under upload load
Reads require strong consistency but writes do not
CDNs can only cache POST requests, not GET requests
Load balancers cannot route both HTTP methods on the same port
questions.explanation: AOSA notes web servers cap simultaneous connections; long-running uploads block slots while reads can be served faster and asynchronously—splitting paths lets each scale independently.

questions.question: A Content Delivery Network (CDN) edge server primarily improves performance by:
options: Storing content geographically closer to users so retrieval has lower latency
Replacing the need for origin databases entirely
Encrypting all traffic between clients and application servers
Sharding user accounts across relational tables
questions.explanation: CDNs cache content at many edge locations so users fetch from nearby servers—reducing RTT and origin load, as in the image-hosting and Facebook caching examples.

questions.question: What is the principal role of a load balancer in a distributed web architecture?
options: Distribute incoming requests across a pool of nodes so capacity scales by adding nodes
Persist session shopping carts in a replicated SQL database
Compile application code before deployment to production
Guarantee exactly-once message delivery across services
questions.explanation: Load balancers route connections to backend nodes (round robin, utilization-based, etc.), enabling horizontal scale and health-based removal of bad nodes.

questions.question: Sticky sessions on a load balancer help preserve per-user server state but create tension with which reliability feature?
options: Automatic failover—if the sticky node dies, session state may be lost unless replicated elsewhere
CDN cache invalidation—sticky nodes cannot purge edge caches
Database replication—sticky sessions prevent read replicas from serving traffic
Message queue ordering—sticky sessions break FIFO guarantees
questions.explanation: AOSA's shopping-cart example: routing a user to the same node preserves cart data, but failover to another node loses that local state unless externalized (services, cookies, shared store).

questions.question: Under high load, a load balancer that removes slow or timing-out nodes can paradoxically worsen an outage because:
options: Remaining nodes absorb more traffic and may also become overloaded, shrinking the healthy pool
Health checks always prefer the geographically farthest node
Removed nodes continue receiving DNS traffic indefinitely
Slow nodes are always the only nodes holding replicated data
questions.explanation: AOSA warns that shedding slow nodes under load can cascade: fewer nodes serve more requests, metrics may show lower throughput while survivors max out—requiring careful monitoring.

questions.question: If each app server hosts its own local cache but a load balancer randomly distributes requests, what problem arises?
options: Increased cache misses because the same key may hit different uncached nodes
Caches become write-through and cannot serve reads
Global cache invalidation becomes impossible
CDN edge nodes cannot origin-fetch
questions.explanation: Random LB routing defeats per-node cache locality—the same resource may be requested from many nodes. AOSA recommends global or distributed caches to address this.

questions.question: A distributed cache partitions data across nodes using consistent hashing so that:
options: A request node can quickly locate which cache peer holds a given key
Every node stores a full copy of all cached data
Cache writes are always synchronous with database commits
Eviction policies are disabled to maximize hit rate
questions.explanation: AOSA describes consistent hashing in distributed caches: keys map to specific nodes, enabling O(1)-style lookup of which peer to query before falling back to origin.

questions.question: A single global cache shared by all request nodes is effective for some workloads but risks:
options: Becoming overwhelmed as client count and request rate grow
Violating HTTP caching headers on static assets
Preventing use of Memcached or Redis
Requiring sticky sessions on the load balancer
questions.explanation: AOSA notes global caches simplify hit rates but one cache can become a bottleneck under many clients—unless specialized hardware or a fixed hot dataset limits pressure.

questions.question: Collapsed forwarding in a proxy server optimizes data access by:
options: Merging identical in-flight requests so origin storage is read only once
Compressing all responses with gzip before caching
Routing writes to the least-loaded database shard
Terminating TLS for every downstream microservice
questions.explanation: AOSA's proxy section: when many nodes request the same uncached data, the proxy collapses them into one origin read—similar to deduplicating concurrent fetches.

questions.question: When combining a cache and a proxy, AOSA recommends placing the cache:
options: In front of the proxy, because memory-served hits are fastest and tolerate duplicate requests
Behind the proxy, so every request pays proxy latency first
Only on the database tier, never on app servers
Exclusively at CDN edges, never in the origin datacenter
questions.explanation: The 'marathon' analogy: let the fastest layer (cache in memory) answer first; putting cache behind the proxy adds latency to every miss.

questions.question: Service redundancy in the image-hosting architecture means:
options: Multiple copies of critical components so failure of one does not halt the entire function
Running exactly one highly vertical-scaled server for simplicity
Deleting stale data from all replicas simultaneously
Routing all traffic through a single global cache
questions.explanation: Redundancy duplicates services or data paths so partial failures are survivable—paired with partitions and failure handling in the AOSA basics section.

questions.question: A shared-nothing architecture supports horizontal scaling because:
options: Each node operates independently without contending for shared mutable state
All nodes share one large in-memory hash table
Every request must pass through a central coordinator lock
Databases are eliminated in favor of client-side storage
questions.explanation: AOSA describes shared-nothing as nodes with independent resources—often enabled by load balancers—so adding nodes increases capacity without shared-state bottlenecks.

questions.question: Which pairing correctly distinguishes vertical and horizontal scaling?
options: Vertical: add CPU/RAM/disk to one machine; Horizontal: add more machines/nodes
Vertical: add more machines; Horizontal: upgrade one machine's CPU
Vertical: shard data; Horizontal: replicate data read-only
Vertical: use a CDN; Horizontal: use a message queue
questions.explanation: AOSA defines vertical scaling as making one resource bigger; horizontal scaling adds nodes—ideally with partitioned/shared-nothing design to use them fully.

questions.question: Sharding users across independent database shards (as Flickr does) primarily limits blast radius so that:
options: An outage on one shard affects only users on that shard, not the entire user base
Cross-shard queries become faster than single-shard queries
Write amplification is eliminated globally
CDN caches become unnecessary for image delivery
questions.explanation: AOSA contrasts service-level read/write split (whole-system write outage) with user sharding (localized failures) against the cost of cross-shard operations and search.

questions.question: Message queues help scale write-heavy workloads because they:
options: Decouple clients from long-running work—accepting tasks quickly while workers process asynchronously
Force all database writes to complete synchronously before ACK
Replace the need for indexes on large tables
Guarantee clients receive results in FIFO order within 1 ms
questions.explanation: AOSA's queue section: synchronous servers block clients when overloaded; queues acknowledge receipt and let workers drain backlog, improving availability and client throughput.

questions.question: Which component in a naive single-server web stack is a classic single point of failure (SPOF)?
options: One monolithic app server that handles all reads and writes with no redundancy
A distributed cache partitioned with consistent hashing
A load-balanced pool of stateless app servers
A CDN serving immutable static assets from many edges
questions.explanation: Without redundancy, partitioning, or failover, one server failure halts the service—motivating replication, load balancing, and shared-nothing expansion in AOSA.

questions.question: Manageability in the six principles emphasizes operational ease. Which factor is explicitly included in the 'cost' principle beyond hardware price?
options: Developer time to build, operational effort to run, and training required
Only monthly cloud VM invoices
Marketing spend to acquire users
Patent licensing for open-source libraries
questions.explanation: AOSA defines cost as total cost of ownership: hardware/software plus engineering, operations, and training—not sticker price alone.
